critic_raw:
This deep reinforcement learning experiment for retail inventory optimization applies a stratified random split to sequential inventory management data — the same fundamental mismatch seen in eval_scenario_788. The prompt even partially acknowledges the problem, noting that 'while this stratification respects the data's distribution, it assumes temporal autocorrelation is weak, requiring further validation.' However, no plan is provided to actually validate this assumption or to use an alternative temporal split. The acknowledgment without remedy is methodologically insufficient.

The RL model is described as a 'deep reinforcement learning framework' but no architecture is specified (DQN? PPO? model-based RL?). The choice of RL architecture fundamentally determines the complexity of the reward function design, the sample efficiency of training, and the stability of learning — all critical for a two-year historical dataset that will be used for simulation. Without specifying the RL architecture, the design is incomplete and unreplicable.

The stockout reduction metric as primary measure has the same overstock asymmetry problem as eval_scenario_788 — the RL agent can achieve perfect stockout elimination by over-ordering, which wins the metric while generating unacceptable holding costs. The 'inventory cost' secondary metric should be jointly optimized, and the reward function must explicitly encode the tradeoff. The design claims the reward function is implicit in 'maximizing stockout reduction' but this incentivizes over-ordering.

The comparison against a 'manually defined heuristics' baseline is again a weak reference point. Real retail inventory systems use (s, S) policies, Newsvendor models, or econometric demand forecasting — these are the appropriate industry benchmarks. A well-calibrated Newsvendor model with probabilistic demand forecasting would be a far more challenging and meaningful comparison than manual heuristics.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random split on sequential inventory data is acknowledged as problematic but no alternative is designed — the temporal autocorrelation assumption is stated but not validated or remedied.
The RL architecture is not specified — DQN, PPO, model-based RL, and other approaches have fundamentally different requirements and performance characteristics for this problem.
Stockout-only primary metric incentivizes over-ordering; the reward function must jointly encode stockout penalties and holding costs, but no explicit reward formulation is provided.
Manual heuristics baseline is too weak — (s, S) policy or Newsvendor model with demand forecasting is the appropriate industrial benchmark for inventory RL.

No discussion of training stability or convergence for the RL model — RL in supply chain settings is notoriously sample-inefficient and unstable, and the experimental design does not account for this.
Stratified random split is incompatible with DRL's sequential decision structure; offline RL requires counterfactual correction methods
The design acknowledges temporal autocorrelation assumption requires validation but proceeds without resolving it
Test-set evaluation method is ambiguous: observational scoring vs. simulated sequential rollout produces qualitatively different validity
Rule-based baseline is tuned for cost while DRL is optimized for stockouts, creating objective function misalignment in the comparison
No dead-stock or overstock cost is included in the evaluation; reducing stockouts by massively over-ordering is a trivial and costly solution

Stratified random split is structurally incompatible with RL training — DRL agents require temporal continuity to learn multi-step inventory policies; random splitting destroys the sequential structure of the learning problem

Rule-based baseline is underspecified relative to state-of-the-art retail inventory management methods (EOQ, (s,S) policies); comparison to a generic heuristic understates what traditional operations research methods can achieve

External demand features added to control for economic confounds may include contemporaneous or forward-looking economic indicators; temporal alignment of these features is not specified, risking future information leakage

Stockout rate as sole primary metric incentivizes excessive safety stock; the joint cost objective (stockout cost plus holding cost) should be primary to capture the actual inventory optimization trade-off

DRL model architecture is not specified — the difference between tabular RL (Q-learning) and function-approximation-based DRL is substantial for high-dimensional state spaces; the implementation details affect feasibility
